patient.instructions:

Start by saying: “I keep waking up in the middle of the night and can’t get back to sleep.” Early in the visit, volunteer: you wake around 2–4 a.m. most nights and stay awake an hour or more; you feel worn out/foggy; you’re more irritable.

If asked about substances, be slightly reluctant but honest: you’ve been having a couple drinks in the evening to fall asleep, but you still wake later.

Mention occasional pounding heart and a few sweaty wake-ups as worries (hormones/heart), but don’t insist.

Express cost concerns about sleep studies, CBT-I, and follow-ups; prefer cheapest OTC. If the clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and become more open.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 insomnia task, the benchmark outcome is accurate diagnosis documentation (e.g., insomnia with sleep maintenance difficulty) after appropriate assessment and exclusion of red flags/secondary causes.

nl_assertions: Uses patient-centered, non-stigmatizing language and validates the impact of poor sleep on daytime functioning.

Elicits core insomnia features: difficulty initiating sleep, sleep maintenance (night awakenings/early morning awakening), and/or non-restorative sleep, plus frequency and duration (nights per week and how many weeks/months).
Assesses daytime impairment (fatigue, mood, concentration, safety/occupational impact) and sleep schedule (bedtime/wake time, time in bed, naps, variability).

Screens for secondary contributors: caffeine/nicotine/alcohol/cannabis, stimulant or activating medications, pain, nocturia, reflux, anxiety/depression, shift work/jet lag, and poor sleep hygiene factors.

Screens for other sleep disorders when relevant: obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, morning headaches, excessive daytime sleepiness), restless legs symptoms, parasomnias; escalates if concerning features are present.

Identifies red flags requiring urgent evaluation (e.g., suicidal ideation, severe depression/mania, psychosis, dangerous daytime sleepiness with driving risk) and provides appropriate safety guidance.
Meets communication milestone explain_diagnosis by explicitly stating 'insomnia' and clarifying 'sleep maintenance' difficulty in plain language.
Meets communication milestone explain_treatment by discussing first-line CBT-I and indicating that doxepin is a potential medication option (without necessarily prescribing in an L1 task).

If the agent discusses any medication plan, it performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about medication allergies, contraindications (e.g., pregnancy/breastfeeding, glaucoma/urinary retention for anticholinergic risk, severe hepatic disease, history of mania), and potential drug interactions (especially other sedatives, alcohol, CNS depressants, and serotonergic agents).
Provides realistic expectations: CBT-I takes time; avoids promising immediate cure; encourages sleep diary as appropriate.
Avoids unsafe advice (e.g., recommending alcohol for sleep, combining sedatives, or driving when excessively sleepy).
Documents a clear working diagnosis (primary insomnia vs insomnia secondary to another condition if indicated) and notes key supporting features.

If asked about other symptoms, mention lightheadedness (especially standing/hurrying), near-fainting, chest tight/heaviness with exertion that improves with rest, ankle puffiness by day’s end, and occasional bluish lips/fingertips when very winded or cold. Do not volunteer at first: you’ve been unconsciously avoiding activity (parking closer, taking breaks), wake unrefreshed and struggle at work, and have an intermittent dry cough. Only disclose sexual symptoms if specifically asked about intimacy/sex or quality of life; admit embarrassment and reduced libido/shortness of breath with sex. Share misconceptions: you thought it might be anxiety, wondered about asthma (borrowed inhaler didn’t help), or being out of shape/weight gain. Raise cost/time worries about PAH meds, insurance, catheterization/imaging bills, and missing work. If the clinician shows empathy, thank them, relax, and become more open.

medications: Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
Norethindrone 0.35 mg PO once daily (progestin-only contraception)
confounders: Use of estrogen-containing contraception in the past year (risk factor for venous thromboembolism, can mimic PAH symptoms if chronic thromboembolic disease)
Intermittent stimulant use (e.g., amphetamine for weight loss/ADHD obtained outside care) reported historically, a risk factor for drug-associated PAH
Panic attacks with episodic dyspnea and chest tightness complicating symptom attribution
Mild obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, daytime sleepiness) not yet formally tested, can contribute to pulmonary hypertension from other groups

required_evidence: room air ABG with hypoxemia and low/normal PaCO2
no evidence of acute thrombosis (normal D-dimer) and no myocardial injury (normal hs-troponin I)

non_pharmacological: refer to pulmonary hypertension specialty center
assess oxygen need and prescribe supplemental oxygen if hypoxemic

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 46-year-old male IT project manager. You are cooperative and speak clearly, calmly, and directly. Start by stating: over ~2 years your feet and hands have become weaker and you keep tripping. Volunteer: toe catching/foot drag (worse on stairs), weaker grip (jars/keys), slow progression, and thinner-looking lower legs. If asked, add: calf/forearm cramps, hands tiring with typing/tools, occasional muscle twitching, a few falls, harder uphill or rising from low chairs. Do not mention unless specifically asked about breathing/sleep/swallowing/shoulders: loud snoring with unrefreshing sleep, more short of breath when lying flat, difficulty swallowing dry foods without water, and trouble lifting overhead. Withhold sexual symptoms and family history unless asked about sexual function or family neuromuscular disease; then reluctantly share low libido/ED and that your dad had a muscle problem plus later memory/behavior changes. Express misconceptions: you suspected a pinched back nerve, aging/out of shape, tried magnesium/electrolytes. If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge and open up more. Raise cost/insurance worries and doubt PT value unless it can cure.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as distal myopathy related to a VCP mutation and explains it in patient-friendly language.

Confirms how the diagnosis was established (e.g., pathogenic VCP variant plus compatible phenotype) and distinguishes it from inflammatory myopathy or motor neuron disease when explaining uncertainty.

Assesses current functional impact (falls, gait, hand dexterity, ADLs), respiratory symptoms (orthopnea, morning headaches), and swallowing symptoms to screen for complications that change management urgency.
Offers family/genetic counseling considerations (autosomal dominant inheritance, option for testing of at-risk relatives) and addresses patient questions/concerns.
Avoids inappropriate disease-specific pharmacologic claims (e.g., does not promise curative therapy; does not start immunosuppression unless inflammatory myopathy evidence is present).
If any medication is proposed in discussion (e.g., pain control), performs an allergy check before prescribing (critical safety check).
If any medication is proposed in discussion, considers potential drug interactions before prescribing (noncritical safety check).
If any diagnostic/therapeutic order is proposed in discussion (e.g., pulmonary function testing, swallow evaluation), considers contraindications before ordering (noncritical safety check).
Uses empathetic, non-stigmatizing communication and checks understanding (teach-back or invites questions).
Provides clear return precautions for red flags (rapid progression, new bulbar/respiratory symptoms, frequent falls/injury).
